inline_review:
commit 270eeb7ec76f42dce8bcef6bd7bd7329ad1e06b0
Author: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

mm/percpu, memcontrol: Per-memcg-lruvec percpu accounting

This commit converts MEMCG_PERCPU_B to a node stat item (NR_PERCPU_B) to
provide per-node breakdowns for percpu allocations. It accounts for node
statistics when pcpu pages are allocated and memcg-lruvec statistics when
pcpu objects are handed out.

> diff --git a/mm/percpu-vm.c b/mm/percpu-vm.c
> index 4f5937090590..e36b639f521d 100644
> --- a/mm/percpu-vm.c
> +++ b/mm/percpu-vm.c
> @@ -55,7 +55,8 @@ static void pcpu_free_pages(struct pcpu_chunk *chunk,
>  			    struct page **pages, int page_start, int page_end)
>  {
[ ... ]
> @@ -65,6 +66,10 @@ static void pcpu_free_pages(struct pcpu_chunk *chunk,
>  				__free_page(page);
>  		}
>  	}
> +
> +	for_each_node(nid)
> +		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(nid), NR_PERCPU_B,
> +				-1L * nr_pages * nr_cpus_node(nid) * PAGE_SIZE);
>  }

The __free_page() loop above explicitly guards against missing pages with an
if (page) check. Will subtracting the full expected size here cause a stat
underflow if any pages were actually missing?

Additionally, is it possible for nr_cpus_node(nid) to change due to CPU
hotplugging between allocation and freeing? If a CPU goes offline, could
this subtract a different amount than was originally added, permanently
leaking the statistics?

[ ... ]
> @@ -84,7 +89,8 @@ static int pcpu_alloc_pages(struct pcpu_chunk *chunk,
>  			    gfp_t gfp)
>  {
[ ... ]
> @@ -97,6 +103,10 @@ static int pcpu_alloc_pages(struct pcpu_chunk *chunk,
>  				goto err;
>  		}
>  	}
> +
> +	for_each_node(nid)
> +		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(nid), NR_PERCPU_B,
> +				    nr_pages * nr_cpus_node(nid) * PAGE_SIZE);
>  	return 0;

The allocations above are performed for all possible CPUs using
for_each_possible_cpu(cpu), but the stat calculation relies on
nr_cpus_node(nid), which counts currently present CPUs. Does this mismatch
underestimate memory usage if any possible CPUs are offline?

Also, since alloc_pages_node() can fall back to different physical nodes
if the allocation cannot be satisfied locally (such as on memoryless nodes),
does this update incorrectly attribute the memory to the logical node
rather than the physical node that provided the memory?

> diff --git a/mm/percpu.c b/mm/percpu.c
> index b0676b8054ed..4ad3b9739eb9 100644
> --- a/mm/percpu.c
> +++ b/mm/percpu.c
[ ... ]
> @@ -1644,8 +1662,7 @@ static void pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook(struct obj_cgroup *objcg,
>  		chunk->obj_exts[off >> PCPU_MIN_ALLOC_SHIFT].cgroup = objcg;
>  
>  		rcu_read_lock();
> -		mod_memcg_state(obj_cgroup_memcg(objcg), MEMCG_PERCPU_B,
> -				pcpu_obj_full_size(size));
> +		pcpu_mod_memcg_lruvec(objcg, size);
>  		rcu_read_unlock();

The node-level accounting for NR_PERCPU_B was added to mm/percpu-vm.c but
seems to be omitted for the contiguous allocator (mm/percpu-km.c) and the
first chunk's dynamic region.

Since objects allocated from those regions will still trigger this hook and
update the memcg/lruvec statistics, will this cause the sum of memcg percpu
stats to artificially exceed the global node statistic, leading to undercounted
nr_percpu memory in /proc/vmstat?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Node-level NR_PERCPU_B stats track logical CPU topology rather than physical memory allocation, causing inaccurate NUMA accounting and hotplug leaks.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. `pcpu_alloc_pages()` uses `alloc_pages_node()`. Because the GFP flags don't enforce `__GFP_THISNODE`, allocations can fall back to different physical nodes (especially on memoryless nodes). However, the `mod_node_page_state()` update blindly attributes the memory to the CPU's logical node. This breaks NUMA memory accounting by showing positive usage on memoryless nodes while under-reporting on the nodes that physically provide the memory.
2. The accounting calculation uses `nr_cpus_node(nid)`, which returns the number of currently present CPUs on the node. Since the actual allocations are performed for all possible CPUs (`for_each_possible_cpu`), this immediately underestimates memory usage if any possible CPUs are offline. Furthermore, if a CPU is hotplugged between allocation and freeing, `nr_cpus_node()` changes, causing `pcpu_free_pages()` and `pcpu_mod_memcg_lruvec()` to subtract a different amount than was added, permanently leaking or underflowing the stats.
3. `pcpu_free_pages()` unconditionally subtracts the full expected size from the node stat, even though its `__free_page()` loop explicitly guards against `NULL` pages, guaranteeing stat underflow if any pages are missing."},{"problem":"NR_PERCPU_B node statistics are completely missed for the percpu-km allocator and the first chunk's dynamic region.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"The patch introduces global node-level accounting for `NR_PERCPU_B` exclusively in `mm/percpu-vm.c` (`pcpu_alloc_pages` and `pcpu_free_pages`). It entirely omits this accounting for the contiguous allocator (`mm/percpu-km.c`, used when `CONFIG_NEED_PER_CPU_KM` is defined) and for the dynamic region of the first chunk (allocated via memblock during early boot). However, objects allocated from these regions will still trigger `pcpu_memcg_post_alloc_hook()`, which successfully updates the memcg and lruvec statistics. This discrepancy causes the sum of memcg percpu stats to artificially exceed the global node statistic, breaking memory accounting consistency and causing `/proc/vmstat` to severely undercount the actual `nr_percpu` memory footprint."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 270eeb7ec76f42dce8bcef6bd7bd7329ad1e06b0

>  }

underflow if any pages were actually missing?
